---
title: "Who's that Pokémon?"
author:
  - Maria Briones 
  - Riccardo Dondi
  - Sayatan Mandal
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: Brite
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-expand: true
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true #to include the chunks on the final document
  warning: false
  fig-width: 15
  fig-height: 8
---

![](/images/pokemon_head.jpg)

# Our mission: catch them all

A very common issue to every parent is how to advice their 10-year-old child when the kid is about to leave on a world-wide trip. There are many important questions to be adressed before the trip, like "How many pokemons there are?", "Who is the strongest?" "How do I catch them all?".

On this document we try to provide some insights on the Pokémon world to help parents to advice their children on how to catch them all. We will be using the Pokémon dataset from the [tidytuesday repository](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/tree/main), which contains information about all the pokemons from the first generation to the seventh generation.

## Data loading and cleaning

First we need to get our R enviroment ready and load the data. We will be using the `tidytuesdayR` package to load the data, which is a great resource for finding interesting datasets to work with.

```{r}
#| label: set-up

#load pacman library to load all the libraries and install if needed
library(pacman)

# Load libraries
p_load(tidyverse, #for data manipulation
        ggplot2, #for plotting
        tidytuesdayR, #to load the data
        forcats, #for factor manipulation
        farver, #for color manipulation
        knitr, #for appealing tables
        kableExtra, #for appealing tables
        skimr, #for data exploration
        viridisLite, #for color palette
        ggthemes #for ggplot themes
        )
```

With our environment ready we import the data and do some cleaning. We will be using the [Pokémon](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-04-01/readme.md) dataset, which contains information about all the pokémon from the first generation to the seventh generation.

```{r}
#| label: data-import

# import data Pokémon from tidytuesdayR
pokemon_df <- tt_load(2025, week = 13)$pokemon_df
```

Our databse contains information about `{r} nrow(pokemon_df)` pokemons studied on `{r} ncol(pokemon_df)` variables, both numerical and categorical.

```{r}
#| label: first-data-exploration
pokemon_df |> skim()
```

Not all of these features contain valuable information for our analysis, so we will be selecting only the ones that we think are relevant:

-   id: the id of the pokémon
-   pokemon: the name of the pokémon
-   type_1: the primary type of the pokémon
-   type_2: the secondary type of the pokémon (if it has one)
-   hp: the health points of the pokémon
-   attack: the attack points of the pokémon
-   defense: the defense points of the pokémon
-   special_attack: the special attack points of the pokémon
-   special_defense: the special defense points of the pokémon
-   speed: the speed points of the pokémon
-   generation_id: the generation on wich the pokémon was introduced

```{r}
#| label: data-cleaning

# drop columns that we don't need and which have many NAs
pokemon_df <- pokemon_df |>
  select(-c(height, weight, base_experience, color_2, color_f, egg_group_2, species_id, url_icon, url_image))
```

One of the first things we notice is that there are some missing values (NA) on the generation_id variable (exactly `{r} pokemon_df$generation_id |> is.na() |> sum()`). After some research on this subset we were able to determine that these pokemons are all spetial edition pokemons like Charizard Mega X or Pikachu Pop Star. 

In consequence, we will be dropping these pokemons from our analysis, since they are not representative of the general pokémon population and they could mislead our insights.

```{r}
#| label: data-cleaning-2

# drop pokemons with missing generation_id
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  filter(!is.na(generation_id))
```

Now we have a clean dataset with `{r} nrow(pokemon_df)` pokemons and `{r} ncol(pokemon_df)` variables.

As we will be performing some analysis on the types of pokemons it's important to get familiar with these variables. During this process we noticed that there are some pokemons that have a secondary type and some that don't (`{r} pokemon_df$type_2 |> is.na() |> sum()` over `{r} pokemon_df  |> nrow()`). For the ones that don't have a secondary type, the value of type_2 is NA. We will be replacing these NA values with "None" to make our analysis easier.

![](/images/types.png){width="60%" fig-align="center"}


```{r}
#| label: data-cleaning-3

#manage NAs in type 2
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  mutate(pokemon = str_to_title(pokemon), #gives some format to Pokémon names.
         type_2 = ifelse(is.na(type_2), "none", type_2) |> str_to_title(),
         type_1 = str_to_title(type_1))
```

# Three insights on the Pokémon world

## 1. Pokémon distribution by generation

Now is time to adress some of our questions: How many pokemons there are in each generation? How powerfull are they? We can get some ideas about these topics by looking at the distribution of pokemons and their most important stats. To simplify our analysis we combine attack and special attack on one single variable and do the same for defense.

```{r}
#| label: pokemon-distribution

# to a better analysis we create to combined variables
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  mutate(comb_attack = attack + special_attack,
         comb_defense = defense + special_defense)
         
#summarise pokemon distribution by generation and main stats
interest_stats = pokemon_df |> 
  group_by(generation_id) |> 
  summarise(n = n(),
            avg_hp = mean(hp, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_attack = mean(comb_attack, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_defense = mean(comb_defense, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_speed = mean(speed, na.rm = TRUE)
)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-poke_distribution
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Pokemon stats by generation"

# Identify generations with the highest number of pokemons
top_gen <- rank(-interest_stats$n) <= 3

#identify the top generation on each stat
top_hp <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_hp) <= 3
top_attack <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_attack) <= 3
top_defense <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_defense) <= 3
top_speed <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_speed) <= 3

turbo=turbo(n=3, begin= 0.8, end=0.99, direction = 1) #3 color from turbo palette

# Create background color vector (only for top 3)
gen_colors <- ifelse(top_gen, spec_color(interest_stats$n, end = 0.5), "transparent") #default viridis
hp_colors <- ifelse(top_hp, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_hp, palette=turbo), "black")
at_colors <- ifelse(top_attack, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_attack, palette=turbo),"black")
def_colors <- ifelse(top_defense, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_defense, palette=turbo),"black")
sp_colors <- ifelse(top_speed, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_speed, palette=turbo),"black")

interest_stats |>
  kable(digits = 2,
        col.names = c("Generation", "Count", "HP", "C. Attack", "C. Defense", "Speed")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
                full_width = F, position = "center") |> #some styling options for the table
  column_spec(2, background = gen_colors) |> #backwround color for 2 column (number of pokemons)
  column_spec(3, color = hp_colors, bold = top_hp) |> #color and bold for hp column
  column_spec(4, color = at_colors, bold = top_attack) |> 
  column_spec(5, color = def_colors, bold = top_defense) |> 
  column_spec(6, color = sp_colors, bold = top_speed)
```

From this we can see that the most populated generations are the 1st, 3rd and 5th. Nevertheless, the most powerfull generation considering all four stats (combined attack, combined defense, hp and speed) is the 4th generation.

## 2. What can we say about types

Pokemons come on a wide variety of types. Are some types more common than others?  These are very important questions as we know that advantages and weaknesses of types are a key element of the Pokémon world. For example, water type pokemons are strong against fire type pokemons but weak against electric type pokemons. In consequence, if we want to catch them all we need to know which types are more common and which types are more powerful.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-type_distribution
#| tbl-cap: "Number of Pokemons by type and generation"

#table for count of pokemon by type and generation 
pokemon_df |> 
  group_by(generation_id, type_1) |> 
  summarise(n = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = generation_id, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(Total = rowSums(across(where(is.numeric)))) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Primary type", "Gen 1", "Gen 2", "Gen 3", "Gen 4", "Gen 5", "Gen 6",
                      "Gen 7", "Total")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
                full_width = F, position = "center")
```

We can see that the most common types are water, normal and Grass. However, these types are not equally distributed across generations. For example, the water type is more common in the 1st generation than in the 4th generation and Normal type is the rarest main type on the 6th generation.

## 3. Dual-type vs Single-type

With this said. Do Dual-type Pokémon (those with a secondary type) consistently outperform single-types in raw power? As they may be less common they might be worth the effort of chasing them. We engineer `is_dual_type` to identify this pokemons and then explore and quantify the gap.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dualtype_stats
#| tbl-cap: "Summary Power Statistics by Dual-Type Status"

df <- pokemon_df |>
  mutate(
    type_2       = na_if(type_2, "None"),
    is_dual_type = !is.na(type_2),
    power_stats  = hp + attack + defense + special_attack + special_defense + speed
  )

dual_summary <- df |>
  group_by(is_dual_type) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    mean_power   = round(mean(power_stats), 1),
    median_power = round(median(power_stats), 1),
    sd_power     = round(sd(power_stats), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

dual_summary |>
  kable(col.names = c("is Dual?", "Count", "Mean Power", "Median Power", "Power SD")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

As we can see, Dual-type Pokémon average **`r dual_summary |> filter(is_dual_type) |> pull(mean_power)`** total stats vs **`r dual_summary |> filter(!is_dual_type) |> pull(mean_power)`** for single-types — a \~35-point (8.56%) gap.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-primary_dualtype_stats
#| tbl-cap: "Mean Power Stats by Primary Type and Dual-Type Status"

type_dual <- df |>
  group_by(type_1, is_dual_type) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    mean_power = round(mean(power_stats), 1),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 5) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_power))

type_dual |> slice_head(n = 10) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Primary Type", "is Dual?", "Count", "Mean Power")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

Dragon dual-types lead all groups at 568 power average. Fire types follows far behind with and average of 490.5 on power stats.

![](/images/strong_c.gif){fig-align="center"}

# Visualizations

## 1. Type proportion on our sample

The analysis from table @tbl-type_distribution can be enriched if we consider the secondary type of pokemons. Considerin the 18 main types and the 19 secondary types (including "None") there are 342 possible combinations we can create a heatmap to visualize the proportion of pokemons by type 1 and type 2 across the total universe of registered pokemons. This will allow us to see which combinations of types are more common and which are more rare.

```{r}
#| label: type-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Normalized proportion of pokemons by types"

# count of pokemon by type 1 and type 2
poke_type <- pokemon_df|> 
  group_by(type_1, type_2) |> 
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  complete(type_1, type_2, fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(prop = n * 100 / sum(n), 
         norm_prop = case_when (prop==0 ~ -2.1,
                                TRUE ~ log(prop))
        )

poke_type <- poke_type |>
  mutate(type_2 = fct_relevel(type_2, "None")) #we make it a factor so "None" appears first

#graph 
ggplot(poke_type, aes(x = type_2, y = type_1, fill = norm_prop)) +
  geom_tile(color = "lightgrey") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#B22222") +
  labs(
    title = "Normalized proportion of pokemons by types",
    x = "Secondary Type",
    y = "Main Type",
    fill = "Norm. Prop.",
    caption = "Normalization is performed as ln(proportion) to better visualize types with low proportions."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

Assuming that each pokémon species distributed homogeneously across the world, meaning that for example catching a Blastoise is as likely as catching a Squirtle (strong assumption), we could say that the most abuntant pokemons belong to the Water-None combination. 

Outside the None secondary type, the most common combinations are Normal-Flying (like Pidgey), Grass-Poison (like Bulbasaur) and Bug-Flying (like Butterfree). On the other hand, there are 42 combinations that have only one pokémon species, like the Ghost-Fairy, Fire-Dark, or Dragon-Electric.

## 2. Distribution of stats by type

We had done a visualization on the distribituion of stats w.r.t the generation but we did not see any changes that made sense as the generation increased.

So we try to see the different stats of different types of pokemons and we can look at the graph and find out the Strongest , Weakest , Slowest or Fastest types of pokemons.

```{r}
#| label: fig_type-stats
#| fig-cap: "Average Stats by Primary Type"

#new interest stats by type
# 1. Aggregate data by Type only
type_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(type_1) %>%
  summarise(
    # Calculate the means of the sums directly
    attack_total  = mean(attack + special_attack, na.rm = TRUE),
    defense_total = mean(defense + special_defense, na.rm = TRUE),
    speed         = mean(speed, na.rm = TRUE),
    hp            = mean(hp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(attack_total)) %>%
  mutate(type_1 = factor(type_1, levels = type_1))

# 2. Pivot (Note: Ensure the names match your new columns)
type_long <- type_summary %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(attack_total, defense_total),
    names_to = "stat_name",
    values_to = "stat_value"
  )

# 3. Plot
ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = type_long,
    aes(x = type_1, y = stat_value, fill = stat_name),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  # Dashed line for Speed
  geom_line(
    data = type_summary,
    aes(x = type_1, y = speed, group = 1, color = "Avg Speed"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  # Solid line for HP
  geom_line(
    data = type_summary,
    aes(x = type_1, y = hp, group = 1, color = "Avg HP"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(data = type_summary, aes(x = type_1, y = speed, color = "Avg Speed"), size = 3, shape = 16) +
  geom_point(data = type_summary, aes(x = type_1, y = hp, color = "Avg HP"), size = 3, shape = 15) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("attack_total" = "#EF5350", "defense_total" = "#42A5F5"), # Updated names
    labels = c("Total Attack", "Total Defense"), 
    name = "Base Stats"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Avg Speed" = "#FFA800", "Avg HP" = "#059142"),
    name = "Trends"
  ) +
  theme_clean() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))+
  labs(
    title = "Average Stats by Primary Type",
    x = "Primary Type",
    y = "Average Value"
  )

```

## 3. Single vs Dual-type stat distributions

Coming back to the Dual-type analysis, we perform a violin chart to visualize the distribution of our summarising power stat across the different primary type categories.

```{r}
#| label: ggplot-viz-1
#| fig-cap: "Power Base Stats by Primary Type — Single vs Dual-typing"

# Order types by median total stats (dual-type)
type_order <- df |>
  filter(is_dual_type) |>
  group_by(type_1) |>
  summarise(med = median(power_stats), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(med)) |>
  pull(type_1)

df |>
  mutate(
    type_1       = factor(type_1, levels = type_order),
    typing_label = if_else(is_dual_type, "Dual-type", "Single-type")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = type_1, y = power_stats, fill = typing_label)) +
  geom_violin(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
    alpha = 0.65, colour = NA, scale = "width"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(group = typing_label),
    fun = median, geom = "point", shape = 21,
    colour = "white", fill = "white", size = 1.5,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.85)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Single-type" = "#4FC3F7", "Dual-type" = "#FF6B35")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(100, 800, 100)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Power Base Stats by Primary Type — Single vs Dual-typing",
    subtitle = "Types ordered by dual-type median. White dots = median.",
    x = NULL, y = "Total Base Stats", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#0D0D1A", colour = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#0D0D1A", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "#1E1E3A"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    axis.text   = element_text(colour = "#9E9EA0"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1, face = "bold"),
    axis.title  = element_text(colour = "#C0C0D0"),
    plot.title    = element_text(colour = "white", face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#7B7B9E", size = 10),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "#13132A", colour = NA),
    legend.text  = element_text(colour = "white"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```

Nearly every primary type shows the orange (dual-type) violin sitting higher than the blue (single-type) one. Dragon dual-types are especially concentrated at the top as we saw on @tbl-dualtype_stats; Normal single-types show the widest spread due to a large pool of filler Pokémon.

# Then who's our pokemon?

Considering our analysis, the most competitive Pokémon is **Garchomp**: a Dragon-Ground dual-type from the 4th generation with a stat power of 608 points, 130 attack and 95 defense.

![](/images/garchomp.png){fig-align="center" width="291"}

*Authors note*: After all of this our analysis, we are still betting on Pikachu. ⚡

# Conclusion

After exploring the Pokémon universe across types, generations, and stat distributions, we can finally offer some battle-tested advice to any 10-year-old setting out on their journey.

**Generation matters — but not in the way you might expect.** While generations 1, 3, and 5 offer the largest variety of species to catch, it is generation 4 that consistently produces the most powerful Pokémon across all key stats (HP, attack, defense, and speed). A detour through the Sinnoh region is well worth it.

**Dual-type Pokémon are worth the extra effort.** Prioritise dual-type Pokémon, pay attention to generation 4, and do not underestimate type synergy.

```{r}
#| label: total-clean
#| echo: false
#| include: false
#| warning: false

rm(list = ls()[ls() != c("colorize", "datawd")])
gc()
```
